title: Surfside residents fear homes could be lost to sea in next storm
full_text:
Jason and Ange Ford have a view many people could only dream of, but looking out across the water from their home at Surfside Beach on the NSW South Coast is no longer relaxing.
"You're just lying there day after day, waiting for your home to fall into the ocean," Ms Ford said.
"We don't have insurance on our home, which will cover tidal inundation, so we could be left with nothing at retirement."
The Fords live on a road that is separated from the ocean by a single sand dune.
Although Surfside Beach is on the northern edge of Batemans Bay, it is vulnerable to large south-east swells, such as those that gouged sand from the area in April 2025 and March this year.
"We have been raising this issue, lobbying for years, and it almost seems like it's been two steps forward, two steps back, because nothing happens," Mr Ford said.
"It's really important that we get [something] done soon, because at the moment there are a lot of houses along that stretch of beach which have little to no protection."
It has been one month since Bega MP Michael Holland travelled to Surfside to meet with up to 40 concerned residents.
Residents say they were assured a meeting between the local council, Transport for New South Wales (TfNSW), and the Department of Climate Change, Energy, the Environment and Water (DCCEEW) would take place the following Monday to discuss the issue.
However, the ABC understands that no formal meeting was held.
Dr Holland's office said in a statement that he "proactively escalated the concerns of Surfside residents to relevant authorities and is involved in, and is encouraging, ongoing discussions."
A statement from the Eurobodalla Shire Council said "no council staff were invited to a meeting on Monday 13 April," while a spokesperson for TfNSW told the ABC they did not attend a meeting, and DCCEEW said no formal meeting was held.
Fear for the future
Surfside resident Craig Lyttle said the back-and-forth had left the community confused.
"There's no indication at all that there's any meeting that's going to happen or any meeting that's got Surfside in the agenda," he said.
"We just want a way forward, and we need something to be done here urgently because the next weather event will inundate our houses again."
Temporary solution
Just over a year ago, a large swell washed over the dune and into several yards and homes.
Emergency works were completed by the Eurobodalla Shire Council in September 2025, which involved pushing sand from the beach bottom to rebuild the dune.
Hundreds of plants were planted to help stabilise it.
But on March 27 this year, a powerful storm hit the region, scouring much of the dune and leaving the houses vulnerable once again.
In a statement, the Eurobodalla Shire Council said sand scraping was a temporary emergency measure that was "assessed and permitted by other agencies before it can proceed".
It said the "latest beach scraping [September 2025] was not a declared emergency, but the NSW Government agreed to the work, which required 50 per cent funding by council".
"Council will continue to investigate avenues for further beach scraping, giving residents time to undertake coastal protection of their own properties, and regularly seek NSW government commitment to continued dredging and sand renourishment of the beach close to shore."
Long-term solution
The Eurobodalla Open Coastal Management Plan (CMP), adopted by the council in 2022, outlined a number of options to manage erosion risk over a 10-year period.
An option suggested for Surfside Beach was sand nourishment during channel dredging in the Clyde River.
In October 2025, Transport for NSW conducted channel dredging and dumped approximately 30,000 cubic metres of sand 600 metres off Surfside Beach, hoping it would wash ashore.
That process is currently being monitored by the University of New South Wales.
In the CMP report, the strategy was assessed as being moderately to highly effective, but it noted that "placement of dredge material directly on the beach or marginally offshore (within 100 metres of shoreline) was required to ensure nourishment of the beach is achieved".
A spokesperson for TfNSW said at the time that it was not possible to put the material any closer due to the large boat in use.
Accelerated threat
Jason Ford said the CMP was not working.
"The acceleration in erosion since the CMP was adopted is clearly visible," he said.
"The dredging happened, but it did not help Surfside."
In a statement to the ABC, the Eurobodalla Shire Council said there were "no current intentions to review the plan" and that "current hazards were already known when the plan was developed and sand nourishment, dune management, and revegetation [are] considered the best options for the area".
summary: Residents of Surfside Beach, NSW, are worried their homes could be lost to the sea due to erosion and lack of effective government action. Despite previous emergency measures, many feel urgent solutions are needed as storms continue to threaten their properties.
heading: General Insurance
key_points: Residents fear homes could fall into the ocean.
Jason and Ange Ford highlight lack of insurance for tidal damage.
Bega MP Michael Holland met with residents but no formal follow-up meeting occurred.
Eurobodalla Shire Council completed temporary dune repairs but erosion remains a concern.
Long-term coastal management plan in place, but residents believe it is ineffective.
ticker_line: Surfside residents fear homes lost to erosion
